en.q: What is the ice point (lower fixed point)?
en.a: 0 °C — temperature of pure melting ice at normal atmospheric pressure.

zhHant.q: What is the ice point (lower fixed point)?
zhHant.a: 0 °C — temperature of pure melting ice at normal atmospheric pressure.

en.q: What is the steam point (upper fixed point)?
en.a: 100 °C — temperature of steam over pure boiling water at normal atmospheric pressure.

zhHant.a: 100 °C — temperature of steam over pure boiling water at normal atmospheric pressure.

en.q: How can you increase the sensitivity of a liquid-in-glass thermometer?
en.a: Use a narrower capillary tube — liquid column length increases more per °C.

en.a: Thinner stem → greater length change per °C → more sensitive.

en.q: What are the steps to calibrate an unmarked thermometer?
en.a: Ice point (0 °C) → steam point (100 °C) → mark levels → divide into 100 equal divisions (1 °C each).

zhHant.a: Ice point (0 °C) → steam point (100 °C) → mark levels → divide into 100 equal divisions (1 °C each).

en.q:
If mercury is replaced with alcohol in a liquid-in-glass thermometer, which statements are correct?
(1) It can measure a higher temperature
(2) It becomes less sensitive to temperature change
(3) It is safer to use
en.a: Less sensitive to temperature change, and safer to use (non-toxic). It cannot measure higher temperatures than mercury.

zhHant.q:
If mercury is replaced with alcohol in a liquid-in-glass thermometer, which statements are correct?
(1) It can measure a higher temperature
(2) It becomes less sensitive to temperature change
(3) It is safer to use
zhHant.a: Less sensitive to temperature change, and safer to use (non-toxic). It cannot measure higher temperatures than mercury.

en.q: What is the formula to find temperature T (°C) from mercury column lengths?
en.a:
T/100 = (LT − L0) / (L100 − L0)

LT = length at unknown temperature
L0 = length at 0°C (ice point)
L100 = length at 100°C (steam point)

zhHant.q: What is the formula to find temperature T (°C) from mercury column lengths?
zhHant.a:
T/100 = (LT − L0) / (L100 − L0)

LT = length at unknown temperature
L0 = length at 0°C (ice point)
L100 = length at 100°C (steam point)

en.q: After heater switched off, why does liquid temperature still rise briefly?
en.a: Heater is still hotter than the liquid → heat still flows in → temperature rises, then falls.

zhHant.q: After heater switched off, why does liquid temperature still rise briefly?
zhHant.a: Heater is still hotter than the liquid → heat still flows in → temperature rises, then falls.

en.q: More powerful heater, same water — effect on temperature-time graph?
en.a: Reaches same final temperature in shorter time (steeper slope).

en.q: What is the law of conservation of energy (heat exchange)?
en.a: Energy can neither be created nor destroyed — only changes form.

en.a: Heat loss from hot object = heat gain by cold object.

en.q: What is the specific latent heat of fusion of ice (l<sub>f</sub>)?
en.a: 334,000 J kg⁻¹ — energy to melt 1 kg ice at 0 °C to water at 0 °C.

zhHant.a: 334,000 J kg⁻¹ — energy to melt 1 kg ice at 0 °C to water at 0 °C.

en.a: 2,260,000 J kg⁻¹ — energy to vaporize 1 kg water at 100 °C to steam at 100 °C.

zhHant.a: 2,260,000 J kg⁻¹ — energy to vaporize 1 kg water at 100 °C to steam at 100 °C.

en.q: How does evaporation differ from boiling in terms of temperature?
en.a: Evaporation: any temperature below boiling point; Boiling: at boiling point only.

en.q: Why does evaporation cool the liquid?
en.a: Highest-KE molecules escape → average KE of remaining liquid decreases → temperature drops.

zhHant.q: Why does evaporation cool the liquid?
zhHant.a: Highest-KE molecules escape → average KE of remaining liquid decreases → temperature drops.

en.q: Three factors increasing evaporation rate?
en.a: Higher temperature, larger surface area, lower vapour density above liquid.

zhHant.q: Three factors increasing evaporation rate?
zhHant.a: Higher temperature, larger surface area, lower vapour density above liquid.

en.q: When measuring l<sub>f</sub> of ice, what is formula using joulemeter?
en.a: lf = E / m where m = mass of water melted by heater only.

zhHant.q: When measuring l<sub>f</sub> of ice, what is formula using joulemeter?
zhHant.a: lf = E / m where m = mass of water melted by heater only.

en.q: Why use crushed ice at melting point in l<sub>f</sub> experiment?
en.a: Crushed ice → good thermal contact; at 0 °C → energy goes to melting, not warming.

zhHant.q: Why use crushed ice at melting point in l<sub>f</sub> experiment?
zhHant.a: Crushed ice → good thermal contact; at 0 °C → energy goes to melting, not warming.

en.q: Why use a control funnel (no heater) in l<sub>f</sub> experiment?
en.a: Measures ice melted by surroundings — subtract from experimental result.

zhHant.a: Measures ice melted by surroundings — subtract from experimental result.

en.q: Ice below 0 °C in l<sub>f</sub> experiment — effect on measured l<sub>f</sub>?
en.a: Measured lf is greater than actual (extra energy warms ice first).

zhHant.q: Ice below 0 °C in l<sub>f</sub> experiment — effect on measured l<sub>f</sub>?
zhHant.a: Measured lf is greater than actual (extra energy warms ice first).

en.q: Heat gained from surroundings in l<sub>f</sub> experiment — effect on measured l<sub>f</sub>?
en.a: Measured lf is smaller than actual (extra mass melted).

zhHant.q: Heat gained from surroundings in l<sub>f</sub> experiment — effect on measured l<sub>f</sub>?
zhHant.a: Measured lf is smaller than actual (extra mass melted).

en.q: Why are air conditioners placed high on the wall?
en.a: Cold air is denser → sinks → pushes warm air up → convection current cools room.

en.q: Why are convection heaters placed on the floor?
en.a: Warm air rises, cold air sinks → convection current.

zhHant.q: Why are convection heaters placed on the floor?
zhHant.a: Warm air rises, cold air sinks → convection current.

en.q: During a daytime sea breeze, what is the direction of wind?

About heat and internal energy, which statements are correct?
(1) Heat and internal energy mean the same thing

en.a: Heat is energy transferred due to a temperature difference. Heating increases a body's internal energy.
